article_title: NASA Names the Artemis III Crew, a Four-Astronaut Team Set to Rehearse Lunar-Lander Docking in Earth Orbit in 2027

content_preview.title: NASA Names the Artemis III Crew, a Four-Astronaut Team Set to Rehearse Lunar-Lander Docking in Earth Orbit in 2027
content_preview.summary: NASA assigned Randy Bresnik, Luca Parmitano, Andre Douglas, and Frank Rubio to Artemis III, a 2027 Earth-orbit test flight to practice docking with SpaceX and Blue Origin landers.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

NASA on June 9 named the four prime crew members and a backup for Artemis III, the next mission in its lunar program, and detailed a reshaped flight plan that will keep the crew in Earth orbit rather than sending them around the Moon. According to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-marches-toward-artemis-iii-mission-in-2027-names-crew-members/), the agency "provided new Artemis III details and announced the four prime crew members and a backup for the test flight," which ...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured 864-word News piece with clear sectioning (Overview, The Crew, What the Mission Will Do, Reaction, What We Don't Know). Technical depth is appropriate; the article correctly frames Artemis III as the now-reshaped Earth-orbit docking test rather than a lunar voyage, and distinguishes it from Artemis II and the 2028 lunar landing (Artemis IV).
editor_notes.source_verification: All 3 source snapshots read from disk and verified. source-0.html.gz (NASA primary release, nasa.gov, status 200) confirms verbatim: the June 9 (Tuesday) crew naming; the four prime crew and roles (Randy Bresnik commander, ESA's Luca Parmitano pilot, Andre Douglas and Frank Rubio mission specialists); Bob Hines as backup; 'first time an ESA astronaut has been assigned an Artemis mission'; the SLS/Orion KSC-to-LEO profile; the 'rendezvous and docking ... one, or both ...' quote; Blue Moon/Starship test articles; the Artemis IV 2028 lunar South Pole framing; Rubio 371 days / Soyuz MS-22 / Sept 21 2022 to Sept 27 2023; Bresnik STS-129 2009 / third flight; Parmitano third flight; Douglas first flight; and both the Isaacman and Aschbacher quotes verbatim. source-1.html.gz (Space.com, status 200) confirms the June 9 JSC announcement, 'Bob Hines' as backup (3 occurrences, zero 'Heintz'), 'mid- to late 2027' window, Artemis II '10-day mission around the moon's far side and back to Earth', and the Isaacman 'extraordinary demonstration ... greatest aerospace companies' quote verbatim. source-2.html.gz (Al Jazeera, status 200) confirms the crew lineup and the 'collecting research and practising in-space docking procedures in preparation for a future Moon landing' quote verbatim.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All specifics trace to a cited snapshot. CRITICAL NAME CHECK PASSED: the article uses 'Bob Hines' for the backup astronaut and cites ONLY the NASA primary release (plus Space.com corroboration) for it; Al Jazeera's erroneous 'Bob Heintz' was correctly NOT used. The article also correctly dropped two single-sourced specifics: Al Jazeera's 'two-week' mission duration (absent from body) and Space.com's Jeremy Parsons New Glenn quote (absent from body). Headline, summary, and Overview lead all rest on the non-bot-blocked NASA primary release (status 200, not an Archive.org fallback). Every external body URL appears in article.sources (no orphan/provenance break). All three internal cross-references (/article/2026-03/03-nasa-overhauls-artemis..., /article/2026-04/01-nasa-launches-artemis-ii..., /article/2026-06/02-blue-origins-new-glenn-...) resolve to existing published articles.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism, no AI self-reference, no editorializing. The 'What We Don't Know' section appropriately flags open questions (precise launch date, one-vs-both landers, New Glenn hardware risk) without speculation.
editor_notes.originality: New topic. No prior published article covers the Artemis III crew naming; the piece is distinct from existing Artemis II, Artemis program-overhaul, and New Glenn-explosion coverage, which it references as context.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, fully sourced submission. Every astronaut name and title traces verbatim to the NASA primary release and is corroborated by Space.com; the top failure mode (the 'Bob Hines' vs 'Bob Heintz' discrepancy) was handled correctly. Clean APPROVE, no corrections required.
